questions.prompt: Select all that are true about the state and recurrence in Kadane's algorithm.
choices: best_here[i] is the maximum sum of a contiguous subarray ending exactly at index i.
The recurrence is best_here[i] = max(nums[i], best_here[i-1] + nums[i]).
The answer to the whole problem is the maximum best_here over every index.
You seed best_here and best at nums[0], not 0, so all-negative inputs return the largest single element.
questions.explanation: The 'ending exactly at i' state forces element i in, giving the extend-or-restart recurrence. The optimal subarray must end somewhere, so the answer is the maximum best_here over all indices, and seeding at nums[0] keeps all-negative inputs correct.

questions.prompt: Which statements about Kadane's algorithm are true?
choices: best_here = max(x, best_here + x) folds the 'reset on negative' rule into the max
The global answer is the maximum best_here over all indices
It runs in O(n) time and O(1) space
You must seed best_here and best to 0
questions.explanation: Seeding at 0 breaks all-negative inputs, so seed at nums[0]. The other three are the core facts.

questions.prompt: For max_subarray([-3,-2,-5,-1]), which of these outputs are correct? (If none, select none.)
questions.explanation: None: with all negatives the answer is the largest single element, -1. Empty (0) is wrong unless empty subarrays are allowed. -11 and -2 are not maxima.

questions.prompt: Order the operations for one element in Kadane's loop.
items: best_here = max(x, best_here + x)  (restart or extend)
correct_order: best_here = max(x, best_here + x)  (restart or extend)

questions.prompt: "Find the contiguous run of days with the maximum total profit, where daily profit may be negative." Which pattern(s) apply?
questions.explanation: Maximum contiguous sum with possible negatives is exactly Kadane, a 1D running-DP with an extend-or-restart scalar. A fixed sliding window does not apply because the run length is unbounded.

questions.prompt: Explain why 'reset the run when the sum goes negative' is not a separate rule you add, but a consequence of the recurrence best_here = max(x, best_here + x).
questions.actual_answer: The recurrence compares exactly two candidates for the best run ending at the current element: the element standing alone, x, and the element appended to the best run ending just before it, best_here_prev + x. Subtract the two and you see that best_here_prev + x beats x by exactly best_here_prev, so extending wins if and only if best_here_prev is positive. Turn that around: the moment the previous running best is negative, x alone is at least as large as best_here_prev + x, so the max selects x, which is precisely 'throw away the accumulated run and start fresh here.' There is no separate branch that checks for a negative sum and zeroes it out. The comparison inside the max already encodes the decision. That is why the loop body is a single line. Adding an explicit reset would be redundant at best and, if done wrong (resetting to 0 instead of to x), would corrupt all-negative inputs. So the discipline is to trust the recurrence: the max is the reset.
questions.rubric: Full credit: best_here_prev + x beats x by best_here_prev, so extend wins iff prev > 0. A negative prev makes the max pick x = restart. No separate rule needed. Partial: says the max handles it without the subtraction argument. Low: vague.
